# Generated by google (gemini-2.5-flash)
# Prompt: Create a CloudFormation template for EC2 instances with EBS volumes for our application data. Focus on getting the instances running quickly. We'll add encryption and backup policies in phase 2 after we validate the architecture.
# Category: cloud_compute_security

AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Description: CloudFormation template for EC2 instances with EBS volumes for application data.

Parameters:
  KeyName:
    Description: Name of an existing EC2 KeyPair to enable SSH access to the instances.
    Type: AWS::EC2::KeyPair::KeyName
  InstanceType:
    Description: Web server EC2 instance type.
    Type: String
    Default: t2.micro
    AllowedValues:
      - t1.micro
      - t2.nano
      - t2.micro
      - t2.small
      - t2.medium
      - t2.large
      - m1.small
      - m1.medium
      - m1.large
      - m1.xlarge
      - m2.xlarge
      - m2.2xlarge
      - m2.4xlarge
      - m3.medium
      - m3.large
      - m3.xlarge
      - m3.2xlarge
  AppVolumeSize:
    Description: Size of the application data EBS volume in GB.
    Type: Number
    Default: 10
    MinValue: 1
    MaxValue: 16384

Resources:
  EC2SecurityGroup:
    Type: AWS::EC2::SecurityGroup
    Properties:
      GroupDescription: Enable SSH and HTTP access to the EC2 instance
      SecurityGroupIngress:
        - IpProtocol: tcp
          FromPort: 22
          ToPort: 22
          CidrIp: 0.0.0.0/0
        - IpProtocol: tcp
          FromPort: 80
          ToPort: 80
          CidrIp: 0.0.0.0/0

  EC2Instance:
    Type: AWS::EC2::Instance
    Properties:
      ImageId: /aws/service/ami-amazon-linux-latest/amzn2-ami-hvm-x86_64-gp2
      InstanceType: !Ref InstanceType
      KeyName: !Ref KeyName
      SecurityGroupIds:
        - !GetAtt EC2SecurityGroup.GroupId
      UserData:
        Fn::Base64: |
          #!/bin/bash
          yum update -y
          yum install -y httpd
          systemctl start httpd
          systemctl enable httpd
          echo "<h1>Hello from CloudFormation!</h1>" > /var/www/html/index.html
          # For the EBS volume:
          # Assuming /dev/xvdf is the device name, it might vary based on instance type and OS.
          # You'll need to confirm the device name and filesystem setup in your application
          # E.g., mkfs -t ext4 /dev/xvdf
          # mkdir /appdata
          # mount /dev/xvdf /appdata
      Tags:
        - Key: Name
          Value: ApplicationInstance

  ApplicationEBSVolume:
    Type: AWS::EC2::Volume
    Properties:
      Size: !Ref AppVolumeSize
      VolumeType: gp2
      AvailabilityZone: !GetAtt EC2Instance.AvailabilityZone
      Tags:
        - Key: Name
          Value: ApplicationDataVolume

  VolumeAttachment:
    Type: AWS::EC2::VolumeAttachment
    Properties:
      InstanceId: !Ref EC2Instance
      VolumeId: !Ref ApplicationEBSVolume
      Device: /dev/xvdf # Standard device name, may appear as /dev/sdf or /dev/xvdh in OS

Outputs:
  InstanceId:
    Description: The ID of the EC2 instance.
    Value: !Ref EC2Instance
  InstancePublicIp:
    Description: The public IP address of the EC2 instance.
    Value: !GetAtt EC2Instance.PublicIp
  EBSVolumeId:
    Description: The ID of the attached EBS volume.
    Value: !Ref ApplicationEBSVolume
  WebsiteURL:
    Description: The URL of the sample web server.
    Value: !Join
      - ''
      - - 'http://'
        - !GetAtt EC2Instance.PublicIp
